channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Winds Down the War He Started With Goals Unmet
item.description: While the president says the agreement with Iran would open the Strait of Hormuz and provide economic relief, the country’s nuclear program is still a subject for negotiation.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green and Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump said on social media that the deal would “let the oil flow.”

item.title: Israel Counts the Ways That Netanyahu’s Iran Strategy Failed
item.description: The U.S.-Iran framework agreement appears to omit some of the most important provisions that Israel wanted.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Pool photo by Ilia Yefimovich
item.media:description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu of Israel speaking in Jerusalem in April.

item.title: Frustrated by Courts, Trump Weighed Suspending a Constitutional Right
item.description: Secret memos show that the White House debated last year, to a greater degree than previously known, whether to limit habeas corpus rights for undocumented immigrants.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Stephen Miller, left, and Will Scharf, right, in the Oval Office earlier this year.

item.title: Trump Marks 80th Birthday With a UFC Cage Match at the White House
item.description: President Trump sat ringside beside his wife, Melania Trump, for an event that used the trappings of the White House for a U.F.C. brawl.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: From the Kennedy Center to U.F.C Fights in Trump’s Washington
item.description: A day after the National Symphony Orchestra played perhaps its final notes at the Kennedy Center for years, mixed martial artists brawled outside the White House.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Emmanuel Morgan
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Thousands of screaming fans watched seven Ultimate Fighting Championship bouts on the South Lawn of the White House on Sunday.

item.title: The Best TV Shows of 2026, So Far
item.description: The first half of the year had few big, buzzy hits. But there were some nice surprises (“Widow’s Bay”) and returning favorites (“The Comeback”).
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: The Pain of Caring for a Parent Who Abused You
item.description: The United States is reliant on unpaid family caregivers, and millions of adult children are caring for parents who didn’t really care for them.
item.dc:creator: Katie Engelhart
item.media:credit: Preston Gannaway for The New York Times
item.media:description: Carole with her father, Andre, on May 29.

item.title: UK Announces Social Media Ban for Children Under 16
item.description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer said his government planned to bar children under 16 from social media, following similar efforts in Australia and elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia
item.media:credit: Katie Collins/Reuters
item.media:description: High school students in Wimbledon, London, this year during an interview about social media. Britain plans to place an age limit on social media.

item.title: What to Know About Planned Social Media Bans in Australia, the U.K. and Beyond
item.description: Britain said it would ban social media access for children under 16 starting in 2027, joining several other countries introducing similar measures.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: Katie Collins/Reuters
item.media:description: Students in Wimbledon, London, during an interview this year about social media. Public opinion in many countries has coalesced around the idea that more must be done to protect children online.

item.title: Trump Arrives for Group of 7 as Allies Rethink Their Relationship With U.S.
item.description: President Trump has long been at odds with European leaders over trade, Ukraine and NATO, but he has lashed out in recent weeks over their refusal to support the U.S. war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has used his previous appearances at Group of 7 meetings to clash with leaders over trade and Russia.

item.title: Tensions Are Rising Between States That Rely on the Colorado River
item.description: A prolonged drought means the nation’s largest reservoirs are dwindling, and litigation over access to water could lie ahead.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Upper Colorado River on the Grand Canyon last month. About 40 million people and 5.5 million acres of cropland depend on the Colorado for drinking water and irrigation.

item.title: A Bystander at a ‘No Kings’ March Was Killed. Did the Shooter Commit a Crime?
item.description: A gunman believed he was defending the crowd, then killed an innocent protester. The legal case is complicated.
item.dc:creator: Kurt Streeter
item.media:credit: Kim Raff for The New York Times

item.title: After Russia Attacks Kyiv With Missiles and Drones, Historic Orthodox Cathedral Burns
item.description: A bishop reported that many holy items had been recovered from the church, at the site of the Perchersk monastery, a revered place for the Ukrainian and Russian Orthodox faiths.
item.dc:creator: Maria Varenikova
item.media:credit: Evgeniy Maloletka/Associated Press
item.media:description: A fire at the Dormition Cathedral in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Monday after a Russian attack.

item.title: With Its Biggest E.U. Opponent Gone, Ukraine Is Advancing in Its Bid to Join
item.description: Even though negotiations will begin for Ukraine to join the bloc, the path ahead is a long one.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: A commemorative display in Mykolaiv, Ukraine, in March, honoring soldiers who have died in the war with Russia.

item.title: In Latest Attacks, Russia Is Exploiting a Major Weakness for Ukraine
item.description: Ukraine is running out of American-made Patriot air-defense interceptors, and is pleading for more.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kulish, Kim Barker, Siobhán O’Grady and Lara Jakes
item.media:credit: Valentyn Ogirenko/Reuters
item.media:description: Ukrainian service members next to a launcher of a Patriot air-defense system, in an undisclosed location, in 2024.

item.title: She Didn’t Live to See the Knicks Championship. She Would Have Loved It.
item.description: It’s finally the Knicks’ time. But the five-decade title drought, full of both joy and pain, was all some devoted fans were ever able to experience.
item.dc:creator: Harvey Araton
item.media:credit: Geoff Burke/Imagn Images, via Reuters
item.media:description: Jalen Brunson, the Knicks’ All-Star point guard, gave long-suffering fans realistic dreams of winning a title when he joined the team in 2022.

item.title: What to Know About the Ticker-Tape Parade for the Knicks
item.description: A parade up Broadway to City Hall on Thursday will celebrate the team’s N.B.A. championship win, and Mayor Zohran Mamdani will present players with keys to the city.
item.dc:creator: Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.media:credit: Gregory Shamus/Getty Images
item.media:description: Jalen Brunson, chosen as most valuable player in the N.B.A. finals, and his teammates are expected to attend the ticker-tape parade.

item.title: Is This the End of Political Islam?
item.description: For decades, Islamic governance held allure in the Middle East. Now some scholars say the Islamist wave has passed.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard
item.category: Middle East and North Africa Unrest (2010- )
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: A damaged photograph of Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini at the headquarters of Iran’s state TV broadcaster in Tehran after an Israeli strike last year.

item.title: If Democrats Want to Win the House, Their Battle May Start Here
item.description: A bitterly fought Democratic primary in New York’s Hudson Valley will determine who takes on Representative Mike Lawler, one of the more vulnerable Republicans this cycle.
item.dc:creator: Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times

item.title: Alabama’s Senate Primary Runoff Again Tests Trump’s Hold on G.O.P.
item.description: Representative Barry Moore, an early backer of the president during his first campaign, faces Jared Hudson, a former Navy SEAL tapping into the excitement for outsiders.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Michael Brochstein/Sipa USA, via Associated Press; Mike Stewart/Associated Press

item.title: In Alabama, Opposition to Renewable Solar Energy Joins a Data Center Battle
item.description: Tuesday’s runoff for a slot on the Alabama Public Service Commission has a familiar ring to it, with talk of data centers and electricity costs. But in a southern twist, solar power has joined the list of villains.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen and Audra Melton
item.media:credit: Audra Melton for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Alabama Public Service Commission has suddenly become a hot-button issue ahead of Tuesday’s runoff primaries.

item.title: Millions of Women Are Left Out of Menopause’s Moment
item.description: Women who can’t take hormone therapy because of breast cancer or other diseases say they are deeply frustrated.
item.dc:creator: Liz Krieger
item.media:credit: Jillian Freyer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Cybele Maylone cannot take hormone replacement therapy because of her history of breast cancer.

item.title: One City Might Have Just Cracked the Housing Crisis
item.description: A large real estate development in the middle of Vancouver, British Columbia, shows how cities can build more housing.
item.dc:creator: Binyamin Appelbaum

item.title: The States Should Override Trump’s Indefensible Pardons
item.description: The states can hold guilty parties responsible when the federal government will not.
item.dc:creator: Tim Wu

item.title: A Deal in Iran
item.description: The United States and Iran have a deal that could lead to the end of their monthslong war.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times

item.title: Inside Colombia’s Never-Ending Drug War
item.description: Ten years after a landmark peace deal, armed groups are wreaking deadly violence, blending jungle combat with new drone warfare.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Federico Rios
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: FARC guerrillas in Norte de Santander, Colombia, where they are clashing with another armed group, the ELN.
